# Introduction
## Statistics
> * Statistics is the science of uncertainty & variability
  * Statistics turns data into information
  * Data -> Information -> Knowledge -> Wisdom
  * Data Driven Decisions (3Ds)
  * Statistics is the interpretation of Science
  * Statistics is the Art & Science of learning from data

 

## Variable
> * Characteristic that may vary from individual to individual
  * Height, Weight, CGPA etc


 

## Measurement
> * Process of assigning numbers or labels to objects or states in accordance with logically accepted rules

::: {#fig-TrafficSignals}
![](./fig/TrafficSignals.webp)

Measurement
:::


 

## Measurement Scales
> * **Nominal Scale:** Obersvations may be classified into mutually exclusive & exhaustive classes or categories
* **Ordinal Scale:** Obersvations may be ranked
* **Interval Scale:** Difference between obersvations is meaningful
* **Ratio Scale:** Ratio between obersvations is meaningful & true zero point


::: {#fig-MeasurementScales1}
![](./fig/TypesofMeasurementScales.webp)

Measurement Scales
:::


 

# Exploring Data with Graphs & Numerical Summaries
## Graphs
## Numerical Summaries

###  Example
> The following data shows the ID, Gender (Male, Female), Age, Race (Mexican American, Non-Hispanic Black, Non-Hispanic White, Other Hispanic, Other/Mixed), BMI, and BMI.Cat (Under Weight, Normal Weight, Over Weight, Obese) from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES). The survey is conducted by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), and data are publicly available at: [https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm](https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm) . NHANES data are reported in well over one thousand peer-reviewed journal publications every year.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
Data <-
  read_excel(path = "NHANES1.xlsx") |>
  mutate(
    Gender  = fct_relevel(Gender, "Male", "Female")
  , BMI.Cat = fct_relevel(BMI.Cat, "Under Weight", "Normal Weight", "Over Weight", "Obese")
    )
Data
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 8,819 × 6
      ID Gender   Age Race                 BMI BMI.Cat      
   <dbl> <fct>  <dbl> <chr>              <dbl> <fct>        
 1 31128 Female    11 Non-Hispanic Black  17.4 Under Weight 
 2 31129 Male      15 Non-Hispanic Black  26.5 Over Weight  
 3 31131 Female    44 Non-Hispanic Black  30.9 Obese        
 4 31132 Male      70 Non-Hispanic White  24.7 Normal Weight
 5 31133 Female    16 Non-Hispanic Black  16.8 Under Weight 
 6 31134 Male      73 Non-Hispanic White  30.6 Obese        
 7 31137 Female    14 Non-Hispanic Black  27.8 Over Weight  
 8 31138 Male       3 Mexican American    16.1 Under Weight 
 9 31139 Female    18 Other Hispanic      29.4 Over Weight  
10 31140 Female    11 Non-Hispanic White  18.2 Under Weight 
# ℹ 8,809 more rows
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(janitor)
Out1 <-
  Data |>
  tabyl(Gender) |>
  adorn_totals(c("row")) |>
  adorn_pct_formatting(digits = 2)

Out1
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 Gender    n percent
   Male 4305  48.82%
 Female 4514  51.18%
  Total 8819 100.00%
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(scales)
Plot1 <-
  ggplot(data = Data, mapping = aes(x = Gender)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "count", width = 0.4) +
  geom_text(stat = "count", mapping = aes(label = comma(stat(count))), vjust = -0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(
      expand = c(0, NA)
    , limits = c(0, 4700)
    , labels = scales::comma
    , breaks = pretty_breaks(12)
    ) +
  labs(
    x = "Gender"
  , y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 2/1)

Plot1
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](2022-05-20_TUF_files/figure-html/Plot1-1.svg){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
Out2 <-
  Data |>
  tabyl(Gender)
Out2
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 Gender    n   percent
   Male 4305 0.4881506
 Female 4514 0.5118494
```


:::
:::




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggchicklet)
Plot2 <-
  ggplot(data = Out2, mapping = aes(x = Gender, y = n)) +
  geom_chicklet(radius = grid::unit(3, "mm"), width = 0.5) +
  geom_text(mapping = aes(label = scales::comma(n)), vjust = -0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(
      expand = c(0, NA)
    , limits = c(0, 4700)
    , labels = scales::comma
    , breaks = pretty_breaks(12)
    ) +
  labs(
    x = "Gender"
  , y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(aspect.ratio = 2/1)

Plot2
```

# Descriptive Statistics
> * Number of Observations
* Measures of Central Tendency
* Measures of Central Dispersion
* Measures of Skewness
* Measures of Kurtosis

 

###  Example
> The following data shows the ID, Gender (Male, Female), Age, Race (Mexican American, Non-Hispanic Black, Non-Hispanic White, Other Hispanic, Other/Mixed), BMI, and BMI.Cat (Under Weight, Normal Weight, Over Weight, Obese) from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES). The survey is conducted by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), and data are publicly available at: [https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm](https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm) . NHANES data are reported in well over one thousand peer-reviewed journal publications every year.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
Plot6 <-
  ggplot(data = Data, mapping = aes(y = BMI)) +
  geom_boxplot(stat = "boxplot", outlier.colour = "red") +
  scale_x_discrete() +
  scale_y_continuous(
      expand = c(0, NA)
    , limits = c(0, NA)
    , labels = scales::comma
    , breaks = pretty_breaks(12)
    ) +
  labs(
    x = ""
  , y = "BMI"
  ) +
  theme_classic()

Plot6
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](2022-05-20_TUF_files/figure-html/Plot6-1.svg){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::



::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
Plot7 <-
  ggplot(data = Data, mapping = aes(y = BMI)) +
  geom_boxplot(stat = "boxplot", outlier.colour = "red") +
  facet_grid(cols = vars(Gender)) +
  scale_x_discrete() +
  scale_y_continuous(
      expand = c(0, NA)
    , limits = c(0, NA)
    , labels = scales::comma
    , breaks = pretty_breaks(12)
    ) +
  labs(
    x = ""
  , y = "BMI"
  ) +
  theme_classic()

Plot7
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](2022-05-20_TUF_files/figure-html/Plot7-1.svg){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(moments)
Out6 <-
  Data |>
  summarise(
    n        = length(BMI)
  , Mean     = mean(BMI)
  , Variance = var(BMI)
  , SD       = sd(BMI)
  , SK       = skewness(BMI)
  , K        = kurtosis(BMI)
  )

Out6
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 1 × 6
      n  Mean Variance    SD    SK     K
  <int> <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1  8819  25.1     57.6  7.59  1.20  8.51
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
Out7 <-
  Data |>
  filter(BMI < 70) |>
  summarise(
    n        = length(BMI)
  , Mean     = mean(BMI)
  , Variance = var(BMI)
  , SD       = sd(BMI)
  , SK       = skewness(BMI)
  , K        = kurtosis(BMI)
  )
Out7
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 1 × 6
      n  Mean Variance    SD    SK     K
  <int> <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1  8816  25.1     55.8  7.47 0.885  4.19
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
Out8 <-
  Data |>
  group_by(Gender) |>
    summarise(
    n        = length(BMI)
  , Mean     = mean(BMI)
  , Variance = var(BMI)
  , SD       = sd(BMI)
  , SK       = skewness(BMI)
  , K        = kurtosis(BMI)
  )
Out8
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 2 × 7
  Gender     n  Mean Variance    SD    SK     K
  <fct>  <int> <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 Male    4305  24.7     50.9  7.14 1.46  14.8 
2 Female  4514  25.4     63.6  7.98 0.994  4.55
```


:::
:::




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
Out9 <-
  Data |>
  filter(BMI < 70) |>
  group_by(Gender) |>
    summarise(
    n        = length(BMI)
  , Mean     = mean(BMI)
  , Variance = var(BMI)
  , SD       = sd(BMI)
  , SK       = skewness(BMI)
  , K        = kurtosis(BMI)
  )

Out9
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 2 × 7
  Gender     n  Mean Variance    SD    SK     K
  <fct>  <int> <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 Male    4304  24.7     48.3  6.95 0.773  4.11
2 Female  4512  25.4     62.6  7.91 0.923  4.07
```

# Correlation Analysis

###  Example
> The following data shows the ID, Gender (Male, Female), Age, Race (Mexican American, Non-Hispanic Black, Non-Hispanic White, Other Hispanic, Other/Mixed), BMI, and BMI.Cat (Under Weight, Normal Weight, Over Weight, Obese) from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES). The survey is conducted by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), and data are publicly available at: [https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm](https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm) . NHANES data are reported in well over one thousand peer-reviewed journal publications every year.




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
Out10 <-
  Data |>
  select(BMI, Age) |>
  var()

Out10
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
         BMI       Age
BMI 57.55196  81.74215
Age 81.74215 504.62626
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
Out11 <-
  Data |>
  select(BMI, Age) |>
  cor()
Out11
```

# An Introduction to Linear Models

## Regression Analysis
> * Quantifying dependency of a normal response on quantitative explanatory variable(s)


 


::: {#fig-PopRegFn}
![](./fig/PopRegFn.webp)

Population Regression Function
:::

 


###  Example
> The following data shows the ID, Gender (Male, Female), Age, Race (Mexican American, Non-Hispanic Black, Non-Hispanic White, Other Hispanic, Other/Mixed), BMI, and BMI.Cat (Under Weight, Normal Weight, Over Weight, Obese) from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES). The survey is conducted by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), and data are publicly available at: [https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm](https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm) . NHANES data are reported in well over one thousand peer-reviewed journal publications every year.



::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
fm1Plot1 <-
  ggplot(data = Data, mapping = aes(x = Age, y = BMI)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_classic()

fm1Plot1
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](2022-05-20_TUF_files/figure-html/fm1Plot1-1.svg){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
fm1 <- lm(formula = BMI ~ Age, data = Data)
summary(fm1)$coef
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
              Estimate  Std. Error  t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 20.3072818 0.116956424 173.6312        0
Age          0.1619855 0.003155804  51.3294        0
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
anova(fm1)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Analysis of Variance Table

Response: BMI
            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
Age          1 116760  116760  2634.7 < 2.2e-16 ***
Residuals 8817 390734      44                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
```


:::
:::




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(emmeans)
fm1Plot2 <-
  emmip(
      object     = fm1
    , formula    =  ~ Age
    , cov.reduce = range
    ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = pretty_breaks(12)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = pretty_breaks(12)) +
  labs(x = "Age", y = "BMI") +
  theme_classic()
fm1Plot2
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](2022-05-20_TUF_files/figure-html/fm1Plot2-1.svg){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggpmisc)
fm1Plot3 <-
  ggplot(data = Data, mapping = aes(x = Age, y = BMI)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "black", formula = y ~ x) +
  stat_poly_eq(
      formula = y ~ x
    , eq.with.lhs = FALSE
    , mapping = aes(
                   label = paste("widehat(italic(BMI))", "~`=`~", gsub("italic(x)","Age",..eq.label..,fixed = TRUE),  "~~~", ..rr.label..,  "~~~",  ..adj.rr.label.., "~~~",  ..p.value.label.. ,  sep = "")
                  )
    , size  = 4.5
    , parse = TRUE
    ) +
  theme_classic()

fm1Plot3
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](2022-05-20_TUF_files/figure-html/fm1Plot3-1.svg){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::


## Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)
> * Comparing means of Normal dependent variable for levels of different factor(s)


 


::: {#fig-ANOVANormalMeans}
![](./fig/ANOVANormalMeans.webp)

Analysis of Variance
:::


 


###  Example
> The following data shows the ID, Gender (Male, Female), Age, Race (Mexican American, Non-Hispanic Black, Non-Hispanic White, Other Hispanic, Other/Mixed), BMI, and BMI.Cat (Under Weight, Normal Weight, Over Weight, Obese) from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES). The survey is conducted by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), and data are publicly available at: [https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm](https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm) . NHANES data are reported in well over one thousand peer-reviewed journal publications every year.




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
fm2 <- lm(formula = BMI ~ Gender, data = Data)
anova(fm2)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Analysis of Variance Table

Response: BMI
            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value    Pr(>F)    
Gender       1   1173 1172.53  20.418 6.304e-06 ***
Residuals 8817 506321   57.43                      
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
```


:::
:::




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
fm2Plot1 <-
  emmip(
      object     = fm2
    , formula    =  ~ Gender
    ) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = pretty_breaks(12)) +
  labs(x = "Gender", y = "BMI") +
  theme_classic()
fm2Plot1
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](2022-05-20_TUF_files/figure-html/fm2Plot1-1.svg){fig-align='center' width=672}
:::
:::



## Analysis of Covariance (ANCOVA)
> * Quantifying dependency of a normal response on quantitative explanatory variable(s)
* Comparing means of Normal dependent variable for levels of different factor(s)



 

::: {#fig-ANCOVA}
![](./fig/ANCOVA.webp)

Analysis of Covariance
:::


 


###  Example
> The following data shows the ID, Gender (Male, Female), Age, Race (Mexican American, Non-Hispanic Black, Non-Hispanic White, Other Hispanic, Other/Mixed), BMI, and BMI.Cat (Under Weight, Normal Weight, Over Weight, Obese) from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES). The survey is conducted by the National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS), and data are publicly available at: [https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm](https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes.htm) . NHANES data are reported in well over one thousand peer-reviewed journal publications every year.




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
fm3 <- lm(formula = BMI ~ Age*Gender, data = Data)
anova(fm3)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Analysis of Variance Table

Response: BMI
             Df Sum Sq Mean Sq   F value    Pr(>F)    
Age           1 116760  116760 2647.6079 < 2.2e-16 ***
Gender        1   1722    1722   39.0430 4.338e-10 ***
Age:Gender    1    270     270    6.1294   0.01331 *  
Residuals  8815 388742      44                        
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(fm3)$coef
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                    Estimate  Std. Error    t value      Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)      20.06946217 0.165999451 120.900775  0.000000e+00
Age               0.15482609 0.004390925  35.260475 6.652534e-255
GenderFemale      0.42441011 0.233467368   1.817856  6.912004e-02
Age:GenderFemale  0.01559778 0.006300190   2.475763  1.331382e-02
```


:::
:::




::: {.cell layout-align="center"}

```{.r .cell-code}
fm3Plot1 <-
  emmip(
      object     = fm3
    , formula    = Gender ~ Age
    , cov.reduce = range
    ) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = pretty_breaks(12)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = pretty_breaks(12)) +
  labs(x = "Age", y = "BMI") +
  theme_classic()
fm3Plot1
```

:::

# R Software

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## R Software
`R` is a free, open-source programming language and software environment for statistical computing, bioinformatics, visualization and general computing. `R` provides a wide variety of statistical and graphical techniques, and is highly extensible. The latest version of `R` can be obtained from [https://cran.r-project.org/bin/](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/){style="font-size: 13pt;"}.
:::

## RStudio
::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## RStudio
`RStudio` is a powerful integrated development environment (IDE) for `R`, and it can be downloaded from  [https://www.rstudio.com/products/rstudio/download/](https://www.rstudio.com/products/rstudio/download/){style="font-size: 13pt;"}.
:::
